source.title: Thalamic amplification of cortical connectivity sustains attentional control

confidence_rationale: Nature paper, optogenetic, with a clean architectural finding: mediodorsal thalamus does NOT relay categorical (rule-content) information into PFC; instead it amplifies local PFC connectivity, allowing rule-specific neural sequences to emerge and be maintained. This makes the rule representation properly local-to-PFC (a compact handle-like local code), but with thalamic functional support. Directly relevant to SD-033a's rule_state architecture and to MECH-262's training-dependent persistence. Confidence 0.78 because while the methodology is excellent, the mapping to REE is partially indirect -- REE has no MD-analogue thalamic substrate and the 'thalamic amplification' role is currently absent.
failure_signatures: Mediodorsal thalamus does NOT carry the categorical rule content -- it only amplifies local PFC connectivity. An architectural reading where thalamus 'feeds rich rule information into PFC' is wrong. The rule content is intrinsically PFC-resident; thalamus only provides the gain.

PFC rule representations require external thalamic sustaining input. An architectural commitment to 'PFC alone holds the rule via recurrent local dynamics' is inadequate. SD-033a's EMA persistence of rule_state is missing this thalamic-amplification factor; persistent rule-holding in biology is a coupled cortico-thalamic phenomenon.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Mediodorsal thalamus sustains rule representations in mouse prefrontal cortex without relaying categorical information. Mediodorsal input amplifies local PFC connectivity, enabling rule-specific neural sequences to emerge and thereby maintain rule representations. Broadly enhancing PFC excitability diminishes rule specificity and behavioural performance, whereas enhancing mediodorsal excitability improves both.
mapping.ree_translation: For SD-033a (lateral-PFC-analogue rule_state) and MECH-262 (rule-selective persistence): the rule representation is properly PFC-local -- a compact handle, not a readout of richer posterior content. But the local representation is sustained by external thalamic amplification, not by PFC-internal recurrence alone. SD-033a's EMA-based rule_state persistence is a coarse abstraction of this; a tighter biological mapping would route rule_state through a thalamic-amplification node that gates which rule-specific PFC connectivity gets boosted at any given time.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The paper studies rule-following in attentional control, not goal-pursuit. Rules and goals are biologically related but not identical -- rules constrain action selection given a context; goals specify desired outcomes. The MD-thalamus result transfers more directly to SD-033a (rule_state) than to ARC-035 (vmPFC goal/value content). Also: REE has no MD-thalamus-analogue substrate, so the 'thalamic amplification gates which PFC sequences get sustained' mechanism cannot currently be expressed in V3 architecture.
mapping.source_context: Mice performed an attention-based rule-switching task while MD-thalamus optogenetic perturbations and PFC single-unit recordings were carried out. Rule-specific neural sequences in PFC were causally dependent on MD input; the MD itself did not encode the categorical rule but provided gain that supported sequence emergence.

confidence_components.notes: Source quality very high. Mapping fidelity moderate because the rule-vs-goal distinction matters and the result transfers more cleanly to SD-033a than to other PFC subdivisions. Transfer risk somewhat elevated because REE has no thalamic-amplification substrate at all, so the mechanism cannot be tested in V3 -- the entry primarily justifies the compact-handle reading of SD-033a's local rule_state.
